consensus_findings.description: The AG-UI event schema requires `threadId` on both RUN_STARTED and RUN_FINISHED (required fields with minLength:1), but the source of that value is unspecified. HarnessEvent.RunStarted/RunFinished carry only `run_id` (harness-event.schema.json lines 17-56), and the mapper signature `map_harness_to_ag_ui(stream: AsyncIterator[HarnessEvent]) -> AsyncIterator[AGUIEvent]` (ag-ui-emitter spec, Requirement "HarnessEvent to AG-UI Event Mapping") has no thread_id input. Design D4 states the server uses the harness's internal `_thread_id`, but neither the harness nor the mapper has a documented hand-off. Implementation cannot satisfy both schemas as-written.

Three artifacts give contradictory contracts for harness exceptions during astream_invoke: (1) harness-adapter spec scenario "astream_invoke emits RunFinished with error on exception" says the harness MUST yield a terminal RunFinished AND the original RuntimeError MUST be 'wrapped or re-raised' (spec.md ~line 131-138 and 193-200); (2) ag-ui-emitter spec scenario "Harness exception surfaces as RUN_FINISHED with error" assumes the harness *raises* ("When the harness stream raises RuntimeError mid-stream") and the mapper synthesizes terminal RUN_FINISHED; (3) harness-adapter scenario "Deep Agents astream_invoke is traced on exception" requires "the original RuntimeError MUST propagate to the caller unchanged." If the harness yields terminal RunFinished AND re-raises, the mapper produces double RUN_FINISHED events. If it only yields, observability decorator never sees the exception. If it only raises, the harness spec's own scenario is violated.

ChatRequest.message has `minLength: 1` but no `maxLength`. Even on loopback, an unbounded user message body can drive memory spikes (a 50MB message ends up in the LLM context, in observability traces, and in any logging). For v1's single-user posture this is not a DoS surface but it is an accidental-misuse surface — a copy/pasted file becomes a server hiccup. Cost of adding a bound is one line.

Requirement "CLI serve Subcommand" says `-H/--harness` defaults to `deep_agents`. This is inconsistent with the existing `run` subcommand pattern, which (per repo convention) resolves harness from persona config. For a persona whose enabled harness is `ms_agent_framework` (the future work persona per CLAUDE.md "What's Not Yet Wired"), `serve` would hardcode-default to `deep_agents` and then fail lifespan with "harness disabled" — surprising for the operator. The CLI spec should defer the default to the persona's configured harness.

Scenario "Response body contains AG-UI events" asserts a TEXT_MESSAGE_CONTENT with delta=='hi' but does not assert the presence of TEXT_MESSAGE_START and TEXT_MESSAGE_END framing. The mapper's bracketing requirement (ag-ui-emitter Requirement "Run Lifecycle Event Ordering") demands START before any CONTENT and exactly one END per messageId — but the web-server-level scenario doesn't verify the bracketing is preserved end-to-end through the SSE response. A regression in the mapper (e.g., omitting START) would pass this scenario.

The web-server spec references "the SSE framing specified by RFC 8895." There is no RFC 8895 defining Server-Sent Events. SSE is specified in the WHATWG HTML Living Standard (Section 9.2 "Server-sent events"), not an IETF RFC. The MIME type `text/event-stream` is registered via IANA but its semantics come from the HTML spec.

RunFinished.error description says "Exception class name (or short error message) when the run failed." Design D8 mandates class name only with explicit reasoning about leakage of file paths, environment-variable values, and secret-bearing exception messages. The schema description's looser "or short error message" wording invites implementers to put exception messages in the error field, which D8 explicitly forbids.

wp-foundation description (line 36) reads "...sse-starlette, optional ag-ui-protocol)" — the "optional" qualifier and the package name `ag-ui-protocol` are stale from the pre-Gate-2 plan revision. Per the revised D5 the package is `ag-ui` (PyPI name) and is non-optional. Inconsistency with proposal.md, design.md, and tasks.md.

Deferred question: wp-web-cli split into wp-web + wp-cli. Recommendation: keep together. The `serve` subcommand is a thin click wrapper around `uvicorn.run(make_app(...))` — implementation is ~30 LOC of CLI plus ~50 LOC of tests, all of which depend on the app factory existing. Splitting creates a new depends_on edge (wp-cli depends on wp-web) without enabling any parallelism — the CLI tests cannot run before the app factory exists. The split would also leave wp-cli with a single Click subcommand as its entire contents, which is too small to be its own package. The current shape (wp-web-cli at 350 LOC, 18 tasks) is moderate and coherent.

Deferred question: rate limiting on /chat. Recommendation: do not add for v1. The v1 contract is explicit: single-user, loopback-bound, local-trust-mode (D12). Adding rate limiting now would: (a) introduce a token-bucket or similar abstraction with state that has no use case in v1; (b) require a follow-up to migrate from per-process to per-user accounting when multi-user lands; (c) provide no defense against the actual threat model (the loopback-only constraint already prevents network-level abuse). Rate limiting belongs in the future change that introduces multi-user / non-loopback support, paired with auth — they're the same design surface.

Deferred question: explicit backpressure requirement. Recommendation: D13's trust of sse-starlette is sufficient for v1. sse-starlette uses an internal asyncio.Queue with a configurable max size (default ping_message + bounded), exposes `request.is_disconnected()` for the response handler to honor, and the existing client-disconnect scenario (5.4b) already verifies aclose() propagation. Adding an explicit backpressure spec requirement would essentially restate what sse-starlette already guarantees — over-engineering for a single-user local SSE bridge. If the future Tauri + web frontend exposes the server to high-rate clients, a dedicated D-decision lands then.

Deferred question: mandatory auth middleware for non-loopback. Recommendation: keep D12's warn-not-refuse posture. The arguments against mandating auth in v1 are strong: (a) SSH-tunneling operators legitimately need to bind 0.0.0.0 inside a container while the host port is loopback; (b) any bearer-token scheme added now is throwaway code when real multi-user auth lands; (c) a warning is honest signaling ("this is local-trust-mode and you are exposing it") without false-security risk. Refuse-without-auth-token becomes the right policy when v2 introduces multi-user — and that policy change is itself a one-line edit (warning → refusal) at that time.

Deferred question: ag_ui version pin in spec deltas. Recommendation: keep pinning in pyproject.toml / uv.lock, not in spec text. Spec deltas describe behavior contracts ("the emitted event MUST conform to AG-UI v0.x"); dependency versions describe build artifacts. Mixing the two means every dependency bump becomes a spec change. The current ag-ui-emitter spec wording "AG-UI protocol v0.x" is the right level of abstraction — a v1.x bump becomes a deliberate spec revision, while a v0.x.y bump is a pyproject.toml diff. Tasks 1.4 captures the pyproject pin correctly.

Deferred question: stable scenario IDs (e.g., web-server.3). Recommendation: keep scenario-name-based references. The existing tasks.md convention ("Spec scenarios: web-server 'Response body contains AG-UI events'") is already tracing-friendly because scenario names are quoted and unique within a spec. Adding numeric IDs would create a maintenance burden (renumbering on insertions) and risk drift between the ID and the name. openspec validate strict doesn't require IDs. If automated test-to-scenario traceability becomes a concern later, it's a separate cross-cutting change.

The plan places `HarnessEvent` in `src/assistant/transports/ag_ui/events.py` while D6 simultaneously says nothing in `harnesses/` may import from `transports/`. Because both `SdkHarnessAdapter.astream_invoke` and concrete harness implementations must type and construct `HarnessEvent`, the written module-boundary rule is impossible to satisfy.

Failure-stream ownership is contradictory: harness specs require the harness to yield `RunFinished(error=...)` on exception and also re-raise/wrap the original exception, while the AG-UI emitter spec also emits terminal `RUN_FINISHED` when the harness stream raises. Implementing this literally can produce duplicate terminal events and unclear tracing semantics.

The class-name-only redaction rule is not consistently reflected in the contracts. `harness-event.schema.json` allows an exception class name or short error message, and the AG-UI emitter spec explicitly forwards `RuntimeError: quota exceeded` faithfully to the client, contradicting D8 and the web-server spec's no-message/no-traceback requirement.

The OpenAPI contract declares 422 responses as `application/problem+json` with an RFC 7807 `Problem` schema, but the plan otherwise relies on ordinary FastAPI request validation, which defaults to `application/json` with FastAPI's validation-error shape unless a custom exception handler is added.

`ChatRequest.message` has `minLength: 1` but no upper bound. Even in single-user local-trust mode, an accidental very large paste can force unnecessary request-body memory use and downstream LLM/harness work before any validation boundary rejects it.

The streaming observability requirement applies to any `SdkHarnessAdapter.astream_invoke`, but the exception-path tracing scenario only covers Deep Agents. MSAF has a success tracing scenario and separate error-propagation scenario, but no assertion that `@traced_harness` emits the failure span with `metadata.error` when MSAF streaming raises.

Deferred question: keep `wp-web-cli` together. The current combined package is acceptable for v1 because the CLI work is a thin `uvicorn.run(make_app(...))` wrapper around the ASGI app, and splitting it would add coordination overhead for modest parallelism gain.

Deferred question: do not require rate limiting on `/chat` for v1. The documented scope is single-user, loopback-default, single-process local trust; rate limiting would be production-hardening beyond the stated v1 boundary.

Deferred question: D13's reliance on `sse-starlette` plus the explicit disconnect/aclose contract is sufficient for v1 backpressure and disconnect handling. No custom backpressure requirement is needed before a remote or multi-client deployment scope exists.

Deferred question: mandatory auth middleware should not be required for loopback or explicit non-loopback binding in this v1 plan. D12's warn-not-refuse posture matches the single-user local-trust contract; real auth should land with a future remote/multi-user design.

Deferred question: do not pin the upstream `ag-ui` Python package version in the spec deltas. The spec should define protocol/event behavior; dependency bounds and the exact resolved version belong in `pyproject.toml` and `uv.lock`.
The map_harness_to_ag_ui function signature in the emitter spec and tasks omits the thread_id argument, which is required to populate the mandatory threadId field in AG-UI events.
ChatRequest.message lacks a maxLength constraint in the OpenAPI contract, which is a standard security gap even for local-trust v1 deployments.
The wp-web-cli work package is overweight, covering both the FastAPI application and the CLI subcommand across 18 tasks and multiple logical layers.
The JSON schemas for HarnessEvent and AGUIEvent describe the error field inconsistently with the redaction rule established in design decision D8.
Spec scenarios lack stable IDs, which degrades test traceability as the project grows.
Rate limiting is correctly omitted for the v1 local-trust single-user scope.
Trusting sse-starlette for backpressure and disconnect detection is sufficient for the v1 local-bridge scope.
The warn-not-refuse posture for non-loopback binding is appropriate for v1 utility while maintaining visibility of the security posture.
